text: The surgeon inserts a Veress needle for laparoscopic cholecystectomy, performing an irrigation test, suction, and hanging drop test. A trocar is introduced supraumbilically, revealing a small visible bubble.
The surgeon begins diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the abdominal cavity through the laparoscopic camera.
The surgeon pushes the small bowel upwards, revealing the sigmoid colon and rectum. The sigmoid colon is then maneuvered towards the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the uterus upward, confirming the absence of adnexal pathology and endometriosis. The uterus is then pushed towards the opposite shoulder.
The surgeon begins dissection approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus, using a 37mm ligature to dissect the round ligament, followed by the fallopian tube.
The surgeon identifies the major salpinx, followed by the ovarian ligament, within the surgical field.
The surgeon coagulates the ovarian ligament approximately two centimeters lateral to the uterus, followed by coagulation of the major ovarian vessels and major salpings.
The surgeon positions the uterus and begins dissection approximately 2.5 centimeters over the broad ligament, preparing to separate the ligament from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the dissection on the right side and pushes the uterus towards the opposite shoulder, ensuring clear visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection on the left side, starting with the round ligament, followed by the dissection of the fallopian tube.
The surgeon completes the dissection of both sides of the adnexal structures, including the ovarian ligament, with visible coagulation and separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon pushes the uterus towards the five o'clock position and separates the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the anterior leaf of the peritoneum anteriorly while the right hand pushes the ureter laterally, creating a proper surgical plane. The uterus is positioned at the six o'clock position as the dissection reaches the midline of the uterovesical fold.
The uterus is maneuvered to the seven o'clock position as the surgeon continues dissection on the right side.
The surgeon separates the right side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, preparing for the next step of bladder separation during the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The surgeon identifies the criss-cross pattern of vessels in the vaginal area while separating the bladder pillar from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon separates the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, focusing on the posterior peritoneum during the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon focuses on the right side of the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, approaching the cervical part of the uterosacral ligament. The peritoneum is pushed laterally, revealing the uterine artery anteriorly and the vein posteriorly, with the ureter visibly sandwiched between them. The structures show minimal skeletonization.
The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the arc of the uterosacral ligament and the cervical part of the uterosacral ligament. Irrigation and suction are used to clean the area.

The surgeon performs coagulation on the ascending uterine artery using a bipolar device, enhancing the efficiency of harmonic and ligature instruments by preventing overcooking after cleaning the surgical area.
The surgeon irrigates the cervical area with saline to prevent star formation and maintain the temperature below 100 degrees Celsius, then proceeds to the opposite side of the surgical field.
The surgeon coagulates the ascending uterine artery and then cuts the main uterine artery on the right side, completing the dissection of this area.
The lumen of the uterine is clearly visible. The surgeon uses the open jaws of the ligature device to coagulate the margins, aiming to stop any potential oozing from the tissue.
The surgeon moves to the opposite side of the surgical field, where the right side of the hip joint has already been dissected. The area is clean with no visible bleeding.
The surgeon uses the open jaw of the bipolar device to manage tissue on the left side, followed by initiating colpotomy with the tip of the harmonic scalpel.

The assistant pushes the colpotomizer into position, ensuring its size is appropriate for effective dissection. The anterior colpotomy is nearly complete, with only a small amount of vaginal epithelial tissue remaining to be dissected.
The surgeon completes the posterior colpotomy, with the uterus now detached and ready for removal. The colpotomizer is pulled together with the uterus out of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to clean the surgical area, ensuring there is no bleeding. The procedure then progresses to the salpingectomy on the right side.
The surgeon performs a salpingectomy on the right side, addressing a small ovarian cyst. The right fallopian tube is carefully dissected and removed, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.

The surgeon lifts the anterior fornix and uses a stone holding forceps to place the tube and ovary into the vagina. The forceps are also used to pull them out, completing this step. A needle is then dropped through the vagina.
The surgeon begins closing the posterior vaginal vault, focusing on the utero sacral ligament.
The surgeon takes a full thickness bite including the epithelium and proceeds to take the anterior loop using intracorporeal continuous suturing technique.

The assistant lifts the bladder peritoneum to expose the vaginal plane. The surgeon approximates the edges of the vaginal fascia and epithelium at the left corner, including the uterosacral ligament, and ties a knot before cutting the suture.
